content.text: The AWS networking and content delivery category provides the foundational plumbing that connects every other AWS service — and your users — together. These services define how traffic flows between the internet and your resources, how multiple AWS accounts and on-premises data centers interconnect, and how content is delivered with low latency globally. Understanding this category is prerequisite knowledge for almost all production AWS architectures.

attrs.alt: AWS networking and content delivery landscape: VPC, CloudFront, Route 53, ELB, Direct Connect, Transit Gateway, VPN, and Global Accelerator, plus the typical request flow and VPC internal structure across three availability zones

content.text: A VPC is a logically isolated section of the AWS cloud where you launch resources in a virtual network that you define. Every AWS account gets a default VPC in each region; production environments should use custom VPCs with carefully designed CIDR ranges and subnet layouts.
— the IP address range for the VPC (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16 gives 65,536 addresses). Choose non-overlapping ranges if you plan to peer VPCs or connect to on-premises networks.

— subdivisions of the VPC CIDR placed in specific AZs. Public subnets route to an Internet Gateway; private subnets route to a NAT Gateway or stay internal. AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet (first 4 + last 1).
— attaches to a VPC and provides the path to/from the internet for resources in public subnets. One IGW per VPC, highly available across AZs.

— deployed in a public subnet, allows resources in private subnets to initiate outbound internet connections (OS updates, API calls) without accepting inbound connections. Costs $0.045/hour plus $0.045/GB processed; place one per AZ for fault tolerance.
— stateful firewalls at the instance/ENI level. Rules specify allowed inbound/outbound traffic; return traffic is automatically allowed. Default deny all inbound, allow all outbound.

— stateless firewalls at the subnet level. Rules are evaluated in order by rule number; explicit ALLOW and DENY are both supported. Less commonly used than security groups; useful for subnet-level IP blocking.

module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.0.0"

  name = "prod"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24", "10.0.5.0/24"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.4.0/24", "10.0.6.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway     = true
  single_nat_gateway     = false   # one per AZ for HA
  enable_dns_hostnames   = true
  enable_dns_support     = true
}
Elastic Load Balancing — ALB, NLB, GWLB

AWS offers three types of managed load balancers, each optimized for different traffic patterns. All are fully managed (no EC2 instances to patch), span multiple AZs, and integrate with Auto Scaling Groups, ECS, and EKS.

— Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS). Content-based routing: route /api/* to one target group and /static/* to another. Native support for WebSockets, HTTP/2, gRPC, and WAF integration. The default choice for web applications and APIs.

— Layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS). Millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency (microseconds). Preserves source IP address. Provides static IP addresses (one per AZ), useful for whitelisting by firewall. Use for non-HTTP protocols, gaming, financial trading, IoT.

— deploys, scales, and manages third-party network appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS, packet inspection). Transparent bump-in-the-wire using GENEVE encapsulation. Routes all traffic through appliances before forwarding.
Amazon Route 53 — DNS and Traffic Management

Route 53 is AWS's authoritative DNS service, offering 100% availability SLA — the only AWS service to do so. Beyond basic DNS, Route 53 provides traffic management policies that make routing decisions based on health, latency, geography, and weight.

— Simple (single value), Weighted (A/B traffic split by percentage), Latency (route to lowest-latency region), Geolocation (route by user's country or continent), Geoproximity (route by distance with bias), Failover (primary/secondary with health checks), Multi-value (return up to 8 healthy records).

— Route 53 health checkers (distributed globally) poll your endpoints every 10 or 30 seconds. Unhealthy records are automatically removed from DNS responses. Use health checks with Failover routing for active-passive DR.

— native AWS extension that points a domain name at an AWS resource (ALB, CloudFront, S3 website, API Gateway) without a CNAME. Unlike CNAME, an Alias can be used at the zone apex (e.g., example.com, not just www.example.com). No extra DNS query charge.
— DNS zones that resolve only within one or more VPCs. Used for internal service discovery (db.prod.internal → RDS endpoint) without exposing names to the public internet.

CloudFront is AWS's CDN with 600+ edge locations (Points of Presence) globally. It caches content at the edge, reducing origin load and latency for end users. CloudFront also provides the entry point for WAF, DDoS protection (Shield Standard, free), and TLS termination.
— S3 buckets (static assets), ALBs (dynamic APIs), EC2 instances, or any HTTP endpoint. An Origin Access Control (OAC) restricts S3 access to only CloudFront, preventing direct S3 URL access.
— different paths can have different cache settings. Cache /static/* for 1 year with immutable headers; cache /api/* for 0 seconds (bypass cache); cache / for 5 minutes.

— run code at the edge to rewrite URLs, add headers, authenticate requests, or personalize content. CloudFront Functions (JS, sub-ms, 10M free/month) for lightweight transforms; Lambda@Edge (Node.js/Python, full Lambda) for complex logic.
— restrict access to content by generating time-limited signatures. Signed URLs protect individual objects; signed cookies protect multiple files (e.g., all content behind a paywall).

As the number of VPCs grows (separate VPCs per environment, per team, per application), connecting them with VPC peering creates a mesh that scales poorly — N VPCs need N×(N-1)/2 peering connections. Transit Gateway (TGW) is a regional hub that any VPC, VPN, or Direct Connect attachment connects to, dramatically simplifying the topology.

— TGW has its own route tables. You can segment traffic: spoke VPCs route to a shared services VPC (DNS, monitoring) but not to each other. Production and development VPCs can be isolated at the TGW level.
— terminate Site-to-Site VPN connections at TGW instead of individual VPCs. On-premises traffic reaches all connected VPCs through a single VPN connection.
— TGWs in different regions can peer over the AWS backbone. All inter-region TGW traffic stays on AWS private fiber.
— $0.05/hour per attachment + $0.02/GB data processed. For high-throughput intra-AWS traffic, VPC peering (free for same-region) may be cheaper for a small number of VPCs.
AWS VPN — Site-to-Site and Client VPN

AWS VPN creates encrypted IPsec tunnels between your on-premises network (or remote users) and AWS VPCs. It is cheaper and faster to set up than Direct Connect, but shares the public internet and cannot guarantee bandwidth or latency.

— connects an on-premises Customer Gateway (a physical or software VPN device) to a Virtual Private Gateway in your VPC or to Transit Gateway. Each connection has two redundant IPsec tunnels to different AWS endpoints.

— OpenVPN-based managed service for remote workers. Individual users install the AWS Client VPN software and authenticate via Active Directory, SAML, or mutual certificate authentication. Traffic tunnels into a VPC.

— VPN is set up in hours, costs ~$0.05/hour + data transfer, and uses the internet (variable latency). Direct Connect takes weeks to provision, costs $0.03–$0.30/hour + port fees, and provides dedicated bandwidth with consistent latency. Many organizations use both: DX as primary, VPN as backup.
VPC Peering vs Transit Gateway vs PrivateLink
Small number of VPCs, free same-region transfer
Many VPCs, segmented network, VPN aggregation
Service endpoint, no VPC overlap concern
Expose a specific service to other VPCs or accounts privately

— VPC peering and TGW require non-overlapping CIDR ranges. Default VPCs all use 172.31.0.0/16; custom VPCs for peered environments must use different ranges. Plan CIDR allocation before creating VPCs — changing a VPC CIDR after the fact requires recreation.

— using one NAT Gateway for all private subnets saves $0.045/hour but creates an AZ dependency. If us-east-1b fails, private subnets in 1a and 1c lose internet access. Deploy one NAT GW per AZ in production.
— circular security group references (A allows B, B allows A) can cause ordering issues during Terraform applies. Use explicit CIDR rules or separate security group rules from the group resources.

— ALB access logs capture every request (source IP, response code, latency, user agent) and are essential for debugging, security forensics, and building audit trails. Enable them to S3 at creation; the cost is minimal (storage only).
— Flow Logs record metadata (not payload) for all traffic to/from VPC interfaces. Critical for security investigations and understanding unexpected traffic patterns. Publish to CloudWatch Logs or S3.
VPC Endpoints — Private Access to AWS Services

VPC endpoints let resources inside your VPC reach AWS services without traversing the public internet, eliminating the need for a NAT Gateway or IGW for those service calls. There are two endpoint types.
ENI with private IP in your subnet
A Gateway endpoint is a route table entry. An Interface endpoint creates an Elastic Network Interface (ENI) inside your subnet with a private IP. Traffic to that service never leaves the AWS network.

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3_gateway" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name      = "com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3"
  vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway"
  route_table_ids   = [aws_route_table.private.id]
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ssm" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.us-east-1.ssm"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.endpoint_sg.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
AWS Direct Connect — Dedicated Private Connectivity

Direct Connect (DX) provides dedicated physical network connections from your data center or colocation facility to an AWS Direct Connect location. Traffic bypasses the public internet, giving consistent latency and higher throughput at lower per-GB transfer costs.
Dedicated (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps) — physical port at a DX location. Hosted — shared capacity through an APN partner (50 Mbps – 10 Gbps).
Private VIF connects to VPC resources. Public VIF reaches all AWS public service endpoints. Transit VIF connects to Transit Gateway for hub-and-spoke.
AWS recommends two DX connections from separate providers to separate DX locations for full redundancy. Use a Site-to-Site VPN as a backup path when only one DX connection exists.
Associates multiple Private VIFs across regions with multiple VPCs, enabling a single DX connection to reach VPCs in multiple AWS regions without peering.
Port-hour charge (dedicated) or connection charge (hosted) plus per-GB data transfer out. More economical than internet NAT Gateway for sustained high-volume egress.
VPC Flow Logs — Network Traffic Visibility

VPC Flow Logs capture metadata about IP traffic flowing through a VPC, subnet, or specific ENI. They do not capture packet payloads — only connection metadata: source/destination IP, ports, protocol, bytes, action (ACCEPT or REJECT), and timestamps.
CloudWatch Logs (query with Insights), S3 (query with Athena), or Kinesis Data Firehose (stream to Splunk/OpenSearch).
Enable at VPC level (captures all ENIs), subnet level, or individual ENI level.
Specify fields like ${srcaddr} ${dstaddr} ${srcport} ${dstport} ${protocol} ${action} ${bytes} to reduce storage costs.
Flow logs are delivered with a ~10-minute delay; not suitable for real-time blocking but essential for forensics and compliance.

-- Athena query: find top talkers by bytes transferred
SELECT srcaddr, dstaddr, SUM(bytes) AS total_bytes
FROM vpc_flow_logs
WHERE action = 'ACCEPT'
  AND date_partition = '2024/01/15'
GROUP BY srcaddr, dstaddr
ORDER BY total_bytes DESC
LIMIT 20;

By default, resources in a VPC use the VPC resolver (169.254.169.253) for DNS. When you need DNS queries to resolve across a hybrid network — from on-premises to VPC private zones or vice versa — Route 53 Resolver endpoints bridge the gap.
An ENI in your VPC that forwards DNS queries arriving from your on-premises network to Route 53. On-premises hosts can now resolve your VPC's private hosted zone records.
Forwards DNS queries originating inside your VPC to your on-premises DNS servers (or another target) based on Resolver Rules. VPC instances can resolve on-premises hostnames.
FORWARD rules specify which domains route to which IP addresses. SYSTEM rules keep the default Route 53 behavior for all other domains.
Works with Route 53 Resolver to block queries to known-malicious domains. Associate domain lists (AWS-managed or custom) with firewall rule groups applied to VPCs.
AWS Network Firewall — Managed VPC Firewall

AWS Network Firewall is a stateful managed firewall service deployed inside VPC subnets. Unlike security groups (per-ENI) and NACLs (per-subnet, stateless), Network Firewall performs deep packet inspection with Suricata-compatible stateful rules.
Inspect TCP/UDP flows, including application layer protocol keywords (HTTP header matching, TLS SNI, DNS). Rules are written in Suricata IDS/IPS rule syntax.
Standard 5-tuple rules (src/dst IP, port, protocol) that run before stateful inspection. Suitable for high-speed allow/deny decisions without payload inspection.
Create a firewall endpoint in a dedicated 'firewall subnet' in each AZ. Route traffic through the endpoint using VPC route tables — similar to inserting an IDS appliance.
Combine with Transit Gateway to route all inter-VPC or internet-bound traffic through a shared inspection VPC, reducing the number of firewall instances needed.
Alert logs (Suricata format), flow logs, and TLS logs can be sent to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Firehose for SIEM integration.

Global Accelerator provides two static Anycast IP addresses that route user traffic to the closest AWS edge location, then carries it over the AWS private global network to your endpoints. This eliminates variable internet routing hops between users and your application.

Global Accelerator health-checks your endpoints (ALB, NLB, EC2 EIP, Elastic IP) and removes unhealthy targets from routing. Traffic weights and endpoint groups support blue/green deployments with instant traffic shifting.
ELB Advanced Features — Sticky Sessions, mTLS, and Connection Draining
Elastic Load Balancing includes several production-important behaviors beyond basic request distribution.
Duration-based or application-based cookies bind a client to the same target for the session lifetime. Useful when application state is local to the instance rather than in a shared store.
When a target is deregistered or marked unhealthy, ELB waits up to 300 seconds (configurable) for in-flight requests to complete before forcibly closing connections.
Mutual TLS allows ALB to authenticate clients using X.509 certificates. Pass-through mode forwards the client cert to the backend; verification mode rejects untrusted clients at the load balancer.
ALB natively supports HTTP/2 on the front-end and can route gRPC traffic. The target group protocol must be set to HTTP/2 or gRPC for end-to-end support.
ALB enables this by default (no charge). NLB disables it by default and charges $0.01/GB for cross-AZ traffic when enabled.

# Increase deregistration delay (default 300s)
aws elbv2 modify-target-group-attributes \
  --target-group-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:... \
  --attributes Key=deregistration_delay.timeout_seconds,Value=60
Networking costs often surprise teams. Understanding the cost drivers lets you architect efficiently.

The dominant cost. Charges apply when data exits an AWS region to the internet. EC2 → internet: $0.09/GB (first 10 TB). CloudFront DTO is cheaper ($0.0085–$0.085/GB) and often eliminates EC2 DTO charges entirely.
Traffic between AZs costs $0.01/GB in each direction. Use AZ-affinity in your load balancer target groups to reduce cross-AZ calls for latency-sensitive tiers.
$0.045/hr + $0.045/GB processed. Replace with VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB (free). Use VPC Interface Endpoints for other services to avoid routing through NAT Gateway.
VPC Flow Logs to S3 vs CloudWatch:
S3 delivery is significantly cheaper for high-volume captures. Use Athena for ad-hoc analysis instead of CloudWatch Insights.

VPC is the foundational network container; every production architecture uses custom VPCs with public and private subnets across at least 2 AZs, an Internet Gateway for public subnets, and NAT Gateways (one per AZ) for private subnets.
ALB (Layer 7, content routing) is the default choice for web applications; NLB (Layer 4, TCP/UDP, static IP) is for non-HTTP protocols or clients that require source IP preservation.
Route 53 provides health-check-aware DNS routing with policies for latency, geolocation, weighted A/B splits, and active-passive failover — all with 100% availability SLA.
CloudFront caches content at 600+ global edge locations, reducing origin load and end-user latency; it also provides TLS termination, WAF integration, and DDoS protection via Shield Standard.
Transit Gateway replaces complex VPC peering meshes with a single hub for up to 5,000 VPC, VPN, and Direct Connect attachments, with full routing table control for network segmentation.
